sections.title: What is Mirror
sections.lead: Mirror is a description language for user interfaces. One syntax for structure, styling, and interactions – that compiles to React.

content.text: Every example below is interactive. Edit the code and see the result instantly.
**1. A Component**
Everything in Mirror is a component. Every component has a name – this is how you reuse it later.

content.code: Button "Hello World"

content.text: Button is the component name, "Hello World" is its content.
**2. Add Styling**
Properties style a component. They are written as name-value pairs directly after the component name. Use background for background color, color for text color.

content.code:
Row gap 12
  Button bg #2271c1 pad 12 24 rad 8 "Short form"
  Button background #2271c1 padding 12 24 radius 8 "Long form"

content.text: background #2271c1 sets the background color. padding adds spacing (vertical horizontal), radius rounds corners. Values are in pixels. Properties can be shortened to three letters: background → bg, padding → pad, radius → rad.
**3. Reuse and Override**
Components remember their styling. Use them again and they inherit everything. Override only what's different.

content.code:
Row horizontal gap 12
  Button background #2271c1 padding 12 24 radius 8 "Hello World"
  Button "Save"
  Button background #c12271 "Delete"

content.text: The first Button defines the style. The second inherits it. The third overrides background to red.
**4. Layouts and Children**
Components can contain other components. Indentation (2 spaces) defines hierarchy. Layout properties arrange children.

content.code:
Tile vertical #3281d1 padding 20 radius 12 gap 8
  Label size 12 "Revenue"
  Value size 28 weight bold "2.7 Mio"

content.text: vertical arranges children vertically, gap adds spacing. Label and Value are children of Tile.
**5. Reusable Components**
Add : after a name to define a template. Templates can have child placeholders that get filled when used.

content.code:
Tile: vertical background #3281d1 padding 20 radius 12 gap 8
  Label size 12
  Value size 28 weight bold

Dashboard grid 3 gap 16
  Tile Label "Revenue" Value "2.7 Mio"
  Tile Label "Customers" Value "40"
  Tile Label "Growth" Value "+12%"
  Tile Label "Users" Value "1.2k"
  Tile Label "Orders" Value "89"
  Tile Label "Rating" Value "4.8"

content.text: Tile: defines the template. Each Tile usage fills in the Label and Value content.
**6. States**
Components can have different visual states. Properties inside a state override the defaults when that state is active.

content.code:
Button background #2271c1 padding 12 24 radius 8 "Hover me"
  state hover
    background #1d5ba0
  state active
    background #174a83

content.text: state hover changes color on hover. state active changes it when pressed.
**7. Click Events**
Events trigger actions. onclick runs when the user clicks, onchange when a value changes.

content.code:
Button background #2271c1 padding 12 24 radius 8 "Click me"
  state hover
    background #1d5ba0
  onclick alert "It works!"

content.text: onclick alert "It works!" shows a message when clicked.
**8. Toggle Visibility**
Actions can show, hide, or toggle elements. Use the element's name as the target.

content.code:
Box vertical #3281d1 padding 20 radius 12 gap 12
  Button #2271c1 padding 10 16 radius 6 "Show/Hide Details"
    state hover
      color #1d5ba0
    onclick toggle Details
  Details vertical #222 padding 16 radius 8 gap 4
    Text "Here are the details."
    Text "They appear and disappear."

content.text: toggle Details shows or hides Details on each click.
**9. Starting Hidden**
The hidden property makes an element invisible at the start. It appears when toggled or shown.

content.code:
Box vertical #3281d1 padding 20 radius 12 gap 12
  Button #2271c1 padding 10 16 radius 6 "Show Message"
    state hover
      color #1d5ba0
    onclick toggle Message
  Message hidden "Hello! Click again to hide."

content.text: Message hidden starts invisible. Click the button to reveal it.
The following sections describe each concept in detail.

sections.title: Components
sections.lead: You've seen that Mirror code is compact. Now let's understand why: everything is a component, and components are defined by usage.

content.text: Every element is a component. The first usage defines it, subsequent ones inherit.

content.code:
Row gap 8
  Button background #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 8, "Next"
  Button "Save"
  Button "Cancel"
  Button "Delete"

content.text: The first button sets background (background), padding, and radius. The following ones inherit this definition. Use color to set text color explicitly.

content.task: Change the color to red (#c14022) and the padding to 12 24.
content.code: Button #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 8, "Click me"

content.text: The example above shows how you can define and display components directly while designing. Later, you might want to separate definition from usage. The only change needed is a colon:

content.code:
Button: background #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 8

Row horizontal, gap 8
  Button "Next"
  Button "Save"
  Button "Cancel"
  Button "Delete"

content.text: The colon makes the difference: with a colon, the component is defined but not rendered. Without a colon, it is rendered. The definition can then be moved to a separate file if desired.

content.task: Define a Card: with dark background and padding, then use it twice.

content.text: Interfaces are hierarchical: a card contains a title and text, a header contains a logo and navigation. In HTML, this means nested tags – opening tags, closing tags, and the actual content somewhere in between. In Mirror, indentation expresses this hierarchy directly. The visual structure of the code matches the visual structure of the UI. No closing tags, no tag soup – just clear nesting through whitespace.

content.code:
Footer padding 16, hor, between, #1a1a1a, radius 8
  Save-Area hor, gap 8
    Button #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Save"
    Button "Cancel"
  Navigation-Area hor, gap 8
    Button #333, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Back"
    Button "Next"

content.text: horizontal arranges children horizontally, between distributes available space between them. Indented elements are children of their parent.

content.task: Add a third button "Delete" to the Save-Area. Notice how subsequent buttons inherit from the first one!
content.code:
Footer padding 16, hor, between, #1a1a1a, radius 8
  Save-Area hor, gap 8
    Button background #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Save"
    Button "Cancel"
  Navigation-Area hor, gap 8
    Button background #333, "Back"
    Button "Next"

content.text: The same result is achieved when components are defined separately:

content.code:
Footer: padding 16, hor, between, #1a1a1a, radius 8
Save-Area: hor, gap 8
Navigation-Area: hor, gap 8

Footer
  Save-Area
    Button #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Save"
  Navigation-Area
    Button #333, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Next"

content.text: These components can then be combined freely:

content.code:
Footer
  Save-Area
    Button "Save"
    Button "Cancel"
  Navigation-Area
    Button "Back"
    Button "Next"

content.text: Since components are not tightly coupled, they accept different children:

content.code:
Footer
  Button "Save"
  Button "Cancel"

content.text: Each usage of a component can be customized inline:

content.code: Button #5ba8f5, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Next"

content.text: The label becomes "Next" and the background changes to #5ba8f5. Properties added at usage override the component definition.
For reusable variants, components can be derived from each other:

content.code:
// Base button
Button: padding 8 16, radius 8

// Variants
Primary-Button from Button: background #2271c1
Secondary-Button from Button: background transparent, border 1 #2271c1
Danger-Button from Button: background #c14022

Row horizontal, gap 8
  Primary-Button "Save"
  Secondary-Button "Cancel"
  Danger-Button "Delete"

content.text: With from, a component inherits all properties from the base. New properties are added or override existing ones.

content.code:
Actions horizontal, gap 12
  Primary-Button "Save"
  Secondary-Button "Cancel"
  Danger-Button "Delete"

content.task: Create a GhostBtn variant with transparent background and a blue border.
content.code:
Btn: padding 8 16, radius 8

PrimaryBtn from Btn: background #2271c1
DangerBtn from Btn: background #c14022

// Add GhostBtn here (hint: color transparent, border 1 #2271c1)

Row gap 8
  PrimaryBtn "Save"
  DangerBtn "Delete"

content.text: Components can define state blocks for different visual appearances. Use onclick toggle to switch between them. System states (hover, focus, active, disabled) activate automatically.

content.code:
Button: padding 12 24, background #2271c1, radius 8
  state hover
    background #3388e8

Button "Hover me"

content.text: See States section for details on defining states, system states, and hover shorthand.

content.task: Add a state hover with a lighter blue (#5ba8f5).

content.text: A base component can contain all possible elements and states. Variants inherit everything and override only what they need.
**The Base Component**
The base button defines three child elements: IconLeft, Label, and IconRight. Both icons are hidden by default. It also defines two states: hover and disabled.

content.code:
Button:
  hor, gap 8, padding 12 24, radius 8, color #2271c1
  IconLeft icon check, hidden
  Label "Button"
  IconRight icon chevron-right, hidden

  state hover
    background #1a5a9e

  state disabled
    color #94A3B8, opacity 0.6

Row horizontal, gap 12
  Button
  Button IconLeft visible, Label "Confirm"
  Button disabled, Label "Disabled"

content.text: **Variants**
Each variant inherits all elements and states from the base. It only specifies what's different.

content.code:
Button: hor, gap 8, padding 12 24, radius 8, color #2271c1
  IconLeft icon check, hidden
  Label "Button"

Primary-Button from Button:
  IconLeft visible

Secondary-Button from Button:
  color #E2E8F0, textCol #1E293B

Ghost-Button from Button:
  color transparent, textCol #2271c1, border 1 #2271c1

Row horizontal, gap 12
  Primary-Button Label "Next"
  Secondary-Button Label "Cancel"
  Ghost-Button Label "Info"

content.text: **Usage**
Variants can be used directly, or further customized at instantiation. The disabled state is inherited from the base and available on all variants.

content.code:
Page:
  ver, gap 16, padding 32

  Primary-Button
  Secondary-Button Label "Cancel"
  Ghost-Button Label "Learn more", IconRight visible
  Icon-Button
  Primary-Button disabled

content.text: Your Card component always has the same children. But what if you want different actions sometimes? Two buttons, three buttons, or an icon instead? Slots let you define where content goes without prescribing what that content is.
Child components with a colon become named slots:

content.code:
Card: ver, padding 24, gap 16, background #1A1A23, radius 12
  Header: hor, between, vertical-center
  Title: size 20, weight 600
  Body: color #9CA3AF
  Actions: hor, gap 8, horizontal-right

Card
  Header
    Badge background #10B981, padding 4 8, radius 4, size 12, "New"
    Icon icon more-vertical
  Title "Project Alpha"
  Body "A new project with great potential."
  Actions
    Button background #333, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Cancel"
    Button background #2271c1, "Save"

content.text: Access nested elements directly by name – no matter how deep they are in the structure:

content.code:
Header: hor, between, padding 12, background #1a1a1a, radius 8
  Left: hor, gap 16, vertical-center
    Logo: width 100, height 32
    Nav: hor, gap 8
  Right:
    Avatar: width 36, height 36, radius 18

// Direct access to nested elements
Header
  Logo background #EF4444
  Avatar background #2271c1

content.text: Names are flat, structure is preserved. This makes components easy to use without knowing their internal hierarchy.
**Creating Multiple Items**
Use - to create new instances instead of modifying existing ones:

content.code:
Menu: ver, width 200, background #1A1A23, radius 8
  Item: padding 8 12

Menu
  - Item "Profile"
  - Item "Settings"
  - Item color #EF4444, "Logout"

content.task: Create a Panel with slots for Header, Body, and Footer.
content.code:
Panel: background #1A1A23, padding 16, radius 8, gap 12
  Header: weight 600
  Body:
  Footer: hor, horizontal-right

Panel
  Header "My Panel"
  Body "Content here..."
  Footer
    Button background #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 6, "OK"

sections.title: Syntax Shortcuts
sections.lead: You understand components and inheritance. Now let's learn shortcuts that make your code even more concise.

content.text: The first one or two numbers after a component name are interpreted as w and height:

content.code:
Row horizontal, gap 8
  Box 80 50, #2271c1, radius 8, cen
    Text size 11, "80×50"
  Card 60, #333, radius 8, cen
    Text size 11, "60"

Row horizontal, gap 8
  Box width 80, height 50, #2271c1, radius 8, cen
    Text size 11, "80×50"
  Card width 60, #333, radius 8, cen
    Text size 11, "60"

content.text: One number sets w only. Two numbers set w and height. You can mix shorthand with explicit properties.

Why this shorthand? Width and h are the most common sizing properties. Making them implicit reduces visual noise while keeping code readable. The pattern mirrors how designers think: "a 300 by 400 box".

content.task: Simplify this code using dimension shorthand – remove width and height keywords.
content.code:
Card width 280, height 180, ver, #3281d1, radius 12, padding 16, gap 8
  Title weight 600, "Project Alpha"
  Text "A description here"

content.text: For Image, the string is the source URL, followed by optional dimensions:

content.code:
Gallery horizontal gap 16
  Image "https://picsum.photos/id/10/200/150" 200 150, radius 8
  Image "https://picsum.photos/id/20/120/80" 120 80, radius 8
  Image "https://picsum.photos/id/64/48/48" 48 48, radius 24

content.text: Use opacity for transparency (0–1, where 0 is fully transparent and 1 is fully opaque):

content.code:
Container horizontal gap 16
  Overlay 80 80 background #000 opacity 0.5 radius 8
  Overlay 80 80 background #2271c1 opacity 0.75 radius 8
  Overlay 80 80 background #fff opacity 0.25 radius 8

sections.title: Tokens
sections.lead: Components centralize structure. But what about values – colors, sizes, spacing? Tokens let you define them once and reuse everywhere.

content.text: The suffix tells Mirror the type. With a typed suffix, the property name can be omitted:

content.code:
// Without suffix – property required
$blue: #2271c1

// With suffix – Mirror infers the property
$blue-color: #2271c1

Row horizontal, gap 12
  Button background $blue, padding 8 16, radius 8, "bg $blue"
  Button $blue-color, padding 8 16, radius 8, "$blue-color"

content.text: Available suffixes:
When you use typed suffixes consistently, your components become cleaner and more readable. Here's a practical example – a styled card using multiple typed tokens:

content.code:
// Design tokens with type suffixes
$primary-color: #2271c1
$text-color: #fff
$muted-color: #94a3b8
$card-padding: 20
$card-radius: 12
$card-gap: 12
$title-size: 18

// Use tokens directly – no property names needed
Card vertical $primary-background $card-padding $card-radius $card-gap
  Title $text-color $title-size weight 600, "Project Status"
  Text $muted-color, "Last updated: Today"

content.text: Without type suffixes, you'd need to specify every property name explicitly: col $primary, pad $padding, rad $radius. The suffixes let Mirror infer the property from the token name.
Tokens hold single values – one color, one number, one string. But what if you want to reuse a combination of properties, like a text style with font, size, and weight together?
That's what Component Definitions are for. While tokens store individual values, definitions bundle multiple properties into reusable components:

content.code:
// Tokens: single values
$primary-color: #2271c1
$card-padding: 16
$card-radius: 8

// Definitions: property combinations
Heading: size 24 weight 600
Subheading: size 16 weight 500 color #94a3b8
Body: size 14 color #e2e8f0

// Use both together
Card vertical $primary-background $card-padding $card-radius gap 8
  Heading "Dashboard"
  Subheading "Welcome back"
  Body "Here's your daily summary."

content.text: Think of it this way: Tokens are your design system's raw materials (colors, spacing, sizes). Definitions are the recipes that combine those materials into consistent patterns (text styles, button variants, card layouts).
Design systems often organize tokens in layers. Base tokens define raw values. Semantic tokens assign meaning. Component tokens connect to specific UI elements.

content.code:
// 1. Base colors
$blue-500: #2271c1
$blue-600: #1a5a9e
$slate-800: #1e293b

// 2. Semantic tokens
$primary-color: $blue-500
$primary-hover-color: $blue-600
$card-color: $slate-800

// 3. Usage
Card $card-color, padding 16, radius 8, gap 8
  Text "Change $blue-500 → all updates"
  Button $primary-color, padding 8 16, radius 6, hover-color $primary-hover-color, "Primary"

content.text: This structure allows changes at any level. Swap $blue-500 for a different shade, and all primary elements update. Reassign $primary-color to a different base color, and the meaning stays intact. Change $button-color independently if buttons need a unique treatment.

content.code:
$btn-color: #2271c1
$btn-hover-color: #1a5a9e

Button: $btn-color, padding 8 16, radius 8
  state hover
    $btn-hover-color

Button "Hover me"

content.task: Add a $danger color token and use it on a second button.
content.code:
$primary: #2271c1
$radius: 8

Row gap 8
  Button background $primary, radius $radius, padding 8 16, "Save"
  // Add danger button here

sections.title: Typography
sections.lead: Any component with a string becomes text. Properties control how it looks.

items: align center – Text alignment (left, center, right)

content.text: color is always text color. background is always background color. Simple and explicit.

content.code:
Box gap 8
  Button background #2271c1 color #FFF "Blue background, white text"
  Title color #2271c1 "Blue text"

content.task: Create a card with a large bold title and a smaller muted description.
content.code:
Card #3281d1, padding 16, radius 8, gap 4
  Title size 18, weight 600, "Card Title"
  Description size 13, "This is a description"

content.text: **Product Card with Image**
Combine images with cards for rich content:

content.code:
ProductCard width 200, background #1a1a1a, radius 12, overflow hidden
  Image "https://picsum.photos/id/96/200/150" w fill, height 150, fit cover
  Info ver, padding 12, gap 4
    Name weight 600, "Wireless Headphones"
    Price color #10B981, "$129"

content.text: Format parts of text inline with *text*:style. The style can be bold, italic, underline, or a custom token.

content.code:
Box gap 8
  Box "This is *important*:bold and this is *emphasized*:italic"
  Box "Click *here*:underline to continue"

content.text: **Custom Style Tokens**
Define a token with multiple style properties and apply it to inline spans.

content.code:
$warning: color #EF4444 weight 700
$highlight: color #22C55E italic

Box gap 8
  Box "There is a *critical error*:$warning in your code"
  Box "This feature is *new*:$highlight in version 2.0"

content.text: Note: *text* without :style is treated as normal text. Use \* to escape a literal asterisk.
icon displays a Lucide icon. Use the icon name from lucide.dev/icons.

content.code:
Row horizontal, gap 16
  Icon icon "home"
  Icon icon "settings"
  Icon icon "user"
  Icon icon "search"

content.text: **Icon Size and Color**
Icons inherit text color from color. Use size to change the icon size.

content.code:
Row horizontal, gap 12, vertical-center
  Small icon "star", size 16, color #71717A
  Medium icon "star", size 24, color #F4F4F5
  Large icon "star", size 32, color #FBBF24

content.text: **Icons in Buttons**
Combine icons with text in buttons. Use horizontal and gap to arrange them.

content.code:
Button hor, gap 8, vertical-center, background #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 6
  Icon icon "download", size 16
  Label "Download"

content.text: **Icon-only Buttons**
For icon-only buttons, use cen to center the icon and set equal w and height.

content.code:
IconButton: width 36, height 36, radius 6, background #3F3F46, cen

Row horizontal, gap 8
  IconButton
    Icon icon "bold", size 16
  IconButton
    Icon icon "italic", size 16
  IconButton
    Icon icon "underline", size 16

content.task: Create a button with a "plus" icon and the text "Add item".
content.code: Button #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Add"

items: size N – Font size in pixels
line N – Line h (multiplier or px)

sections.title: Text
sections.lead: For longer text content, Mirror provides a special syntax with formatting tokens for headings, paragraphs, and inline styles.

content.text: Use single quotes for text that spans multiple lines:

content.code:
text
  '$h2 Welcome

   $p This paragraph spans
   multiple lines.

   $p Another paragraph.'

content.text: Block tokens start a line and style all following text:

content.code:
text
  '$h2 Getting Started

   $lead Mirror is a DSL for UI.

   $p Key features:
   $li Simple syntax
   $li Live preview'

content.text: Inline tokens use brackets to format specific phrases:

content.code:
text
  '$p This has $b[bold] and $i[italic] text.

   $p Visit $link[our site](https://example.com).'

sections.title: Layout
sections.lead: You know how to style text. Now let's arrange elements in space – rows, columns, grids, and alignment.

content.text: By default, children stack vertically. Use horizontal for horizontal layout.

content.code:
Menu
  Item "Dashboard"
  Item "Projects"
  Item "Settings"

Tabs hor
  Tab "Overview"
  Tab "Details"
  Tab "History"

content.text: Use vertical to explicitly set vertical layout, useful when overriding an inherited horizontal layout.
gap sets the space between children. Without gap, elements touch each other.

content.code:
Actions horizontal, gap 12
  Button background #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Save"
  Button "Cancel"
  Button "Delete"

content.task: Create a vertical menu with 16px gap between items.
content.code:
Menu ver
  Item #27272A, padding 12 16, radius 6, "Dashboard"
  Item "Projects"
  Item "Settings"

content.text: Control how children align within their container.

items: horizontal-left / horizontal-center / horizontal-right – Horizontal: left, center, right
vertical-top / vertical-center / vertical-bottom – Vertical: top, center, bottom

content.code:
Header hor, vertical-center, gap 12, padding 16, background #2271c1
  Logo weight 600, "App"
  Status color #22c55e, width 8, height 8, radius 4

content.text: between distributes space between children, pushing them apart. Useful for headers with logo and navigation, or footers with actions on both sides.

content.code:
Header hor, between, vertical-center, padding 16, background #2271c1
  Logo weight 600, "Mirror"
  Nav hor, gap 16
    Link "Docs"
    Link "GitHub"

content.text: Use between for space-between distribution along the main axis.

content.task: Create a footer with "Back" on the left and "Next" on the right.
content.code:
Footer hor, padding 16, #3281d1, radius 8
  Button #27272A, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Back"
  Button #2271c1, "Next"

content.text: **Hero Section with Image**
Combine layout, image, and text for a hero banner:

content.code:
Hero hor, between, vertical-center, background #1a1a1a, padding 32, radius 16, gap 24
  Content ver, gap 12
    Title size 24, weight bold, "Build faster"
    Text color #888, "Design and prototype in one tool."
    Button background #2271c1, padding 12 24, radius 8, "Get Started"
  Image "https://picsum.photos/id/180/200/150" radius 12

content.text: wrap allows children to wrap to the next line when they don't fit. Essential for tag lists, galleries, or responsive layouts.

content.code:
Tags hor, wrap, gap 8
  Tag background #27272A, padding 4 8, radius 4, "React"
  Tag "TypeScript"
  Tag "Node.js"
  Tag "GraphQL"
  Tag "PostgreSQL"
  Tag "Docker"

content.text: grow makes an element expand to fill available space. shrink 0 prevents an element from shrinking.

content.code:
SearchBar hor, gap 8, padding 8, color #2271c1, radius 8
  Input grow, background #27272A, padding 8 12, radius 4, placeholder "Search..."
  Button shrink 0, background #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 4, "Go"

content.text: Common pattern: sidebar with fixed width, main content that grows.

content.code:
Layout hor, gap 16
  Sidebar width 200, shrink 0
  Main grow

content.text: grid creates a CSS Grid layout. Specify the number of columns or explicit column widths.
**Equal Columns**

content.code:
Cards grid 3, gap 16
  Card background #2271c1, padding 16, radius 8, "One"
  Card "Two"
  Card "Three"

content.text: **Custom Column Widths**

content.code:
Layout grid 250 auto, gap 16
  Sidebar background #2271c1, padding 16, radius 8, "Sidebar (250px)"
  Main background #27272A, padding 16, radius 8, "Main (auto)"

content.text: **Percentage Widths**

content.code:
Layout grid 30% 70%, gap 16
  Left #2271c1, padding 16, radius 8, cen, "30%"
  Right #333, padding 16, radius 8, cen, "70%"

content.text: **Auto-fill Grid**
grid auto 200 creates as many 200px columns as fit, automatically wrapping.

content.code:
Gallery grid auto 150, gap 12
  Image "https://picsum.photos/id/10/150/100" radius 8
  Image "https://picsum.photos/id/20/150/100" radius 8
  Image "https://picsum.photos/id/30/150/100" radius 8
  Image "https://picsum.photos/id/40/150/100" radius 8
  Image "https://picsum.photos/id/50/150/100" radius 8

content.task: Create a 2x2 grid of cards with 12px gap.
content.code:
Cards
  Card #3281d1, padding 16, radius 8, "Card 1"
  Card "Card 2"
  Card "Card 3"
  Card "Card 4"

content.text: Make containers scrollable when content overflows. Essential for feeds, lists, and carousels.
**Vertical Scroll**
Use scroll for vertically scrollable content like chat messages or long lists:

content.code:
ChatFeed height 150, scroll, ver, gap 8, #1a1a1a, padding 8, radius 8
  Message #333, padding 12, radius 8, "Hey, how are you?"
  Message #2271c1, padding 12, radius 8, "I'm good, thanks!"
  Message #333, padding 12, radius 8, "Want to grab lunch?"
  Message #2271c1, padding 12, radius 8, "Sure, sounds great!"
  Message #333, padding 12, radius 8, "Meet at 12:30?"

content.text: **Horizontal Carousel**
scroll-horizontal enables horizontal scrolling. Add snap to make items snap into place:

content.code:
Carousel scroll-horizontal, snap, hor, gap 16
  Slide 280 180, color #1A1A23, radius 12, shrink 0, cen, "Slide 1"
  Slide "Slide 2"
  Slide "Slide 3"

content.text: **Both Directions**
scroll-both allows scrolling in both directions – useful for maps or large canvases:

content.code:
MapView width full, height 150, scroll-both, #1a1a1a, radius 8
  Canvas 600 400, #222
    Pin 50 80, #EF4444, width 20, height 20, radius 10
    Pin 200 150, #10B981, width 20, height 20, radius 10
    Pin 400 100, #2271c1, width 20, height 20, radius 10
    Text 100 200, color #666, "Scroll to explore →"

content.task: Create a horizontal carousel of 4 cards with snap scrolling. Each card should be 220px wide.
content.code:
Carousel hor, gap 12
  Card 220 140, #3281d1, radius 8, shrink 0, cen, "1"
  Card "2"
  Card "3"
  Card "4"

content.text: Use stacked to layer children on top of each other. Perfect for text overlays on images, badges, or loading states.

content.code:
ImageCard stacked, width 300, height 200, radius 12, overflow hidden
  Image "https://picsum.photos/id/15/300/200" fit cover
  Overlay ver, horizontal-left, vertical-bottom, padding 16, gap 4
    Title weight 600, "Mountain View"
    Location color #ccc, size 13, "Swiss Alps"

content.text: All children occupy the same space. Use alignment (horizontal-left, vertical-bottom) and padding to position the overlay content.
**Badge Overlay**

content.code:
Avatar stacked, 80 80
  Image "https://i.pravatar.cc/80" radius 40
  Badge background #10B981, 20 20, radius 10, vertical-bottom, horizontal-right

content.text: **Loading State**

content.code:
Card stacked, 200 120, background #1a1a1a, radius 8
  Content ver, cen, gap 8
    Title weight 600, "Dashboard"
    Text color #888, size 13, "Loading data..."
  Loader background #00000080, cen
    Text "⏳"

items: First child is the bottom layer, last child is on top
Use overflow hidden on parent to clip children
Combine with vertical-top/cen/b and horizontal-left/cen/r to position layers
Add semi-transparent backgrounds (#00000080) for overlay effects
grid auto N – Auto-fill with min Npx
stacked – Layer children on top of each other

sections.title: Forms
sections.lead: With components, tokens, typography, and layout you can build any static UI. But users need to input data. That's where form elements come in.

content.text: Mirror supports these HTML primitives: Input, Textarea, Button, Image, and Link.
Add a name after the primitive keyword to create a referenceable component. The syntax is: Primitive Name: properties

content.code:
Input Email: "Enter email", type email
Input Password: "Password", type password

LoginForm color #2271c1, padding 24, radius 12, gap 16, width 300
  Email
  Password
  Button background #2271c1, padding 12 24, radius 6, "Login"

content.text: Now you can reference these by name: Email.value, Password.value.
Use the type property to specify the input type.

content.code:
Input Username: "Username", type text
Input Email: "Email", type email
Input Password: "Password", type password
Input Age: "Age", type number

Form gap 12, width 280
  Username
  Email
  Password
  Age

content.text: For multi-line text input, use Textarea.

content.code:
Textarea Bio: "Tell us about yourself", rows 4

ProfileForm gap 12, width 300
  Bio

content.text: Apply the same styling properties as any other component.

content.code:
Input StyledInput: padding 12 16, background #0a0a0f, border 2 #3B82F6, radius 8

Form gap 12
  StyledInput "Highlighted input"

content.text: Display images with Image. The first string is the source URL:

content.code: Image "https://picsum.photos/id/42/300/200"

content.text: **Dimensions**
Numbers after the URL set width and height. Add radius for rounded corners:

content.code:
Row horizontal, gap 16
  Image "https://picsum.photos/id/64/80/80" 80 80, radius 8
  Image "https://picsum.photos/id/64/80/80" 80 80, radius 40

content.text: **Object Fit**
Use fit to control how images fill their container:

items: fit cover – Fill container, crop if needed (default for most cases)
fit contain – Fit entire image, may leave space
fit fill – Stretch to fill (may distort)

content.code:
Row horizontal, gap 16
  Box ver, gap 4
    Image "https://picsum.photos/id/29/200/300" 100 100, radius 8, fit cover
    Text size 11, color #888, "cover"
  Box ver, gap 4
    Image "https://picsum.photos/id/29/200/300" 100 100, radius 8, fit contain, background #333
    Text size 11, color #888, "contain"

content.text: **Named Images**
Create reusable image definitions with Image Name::

content.code:
Image Avatar: 48 48, radius 24, fit cover

UserRow horizontal, gap 12, vertical-center, background #1a1a1a, padding 12, radius 8
  Avatar "https://i.pravatar.cc/48?u=1"
  Text "Alice"

content.text: Create clickable links with Link. The first string is the URL, followed by the label:

content.code:
Nav hor, gap 16
  Link "#home", "Home"
  Link "#about", color #888, "About"
  Link "#contact", color #888, "Contact"

content.task: Create a user profile card with avatar, name, and a "View Profile" link.
content.code:
Image Avatar: 64 64, radius 32, fit cover

ProfileCard hor, gap 16, vertical-center, background #1a1a1a, padding 16, radius 12
  Avatar "https://i.pravatar.cc/64"
  Info ver, gap 4
    Name weight 600, "Alice Chen"
    Link "#profile", color #3B82F6, size 13, "View Profile"

items: Input named Name: – Create named input
Textarea named Name: – Create named textarea
Image named Name: – Create named image
Link named Name: – Create named link
Button named Name: – Create named button

sections.title: Interactivity
sections.lead: Everything so far has been static – components that look good but don't respond to users. Now we'll bring them to life.

content.text: Let's start with the simplest possible interaction: a button that does something when you click it.

content.code: Button background #2271c1 padding 12 radius 8 onclick toggle "Click me"

content.text: Click the button. It toggles between two states. Let's break down what's happening:

items: toggle – the action: "switch to the next state"

content.text: But wait – what states? We haven't defined any! When you don't define states explicitly, Mirror creates two default states for toggle: a "default" state and an "active" state. The button just gets a subtle visual change.
Let's make it more interesting by defining our own states:

content.code:
Button: padding 12 radius 8 cursor pointer
  state off
    background #333
    "Light Mode"
  state on
    background #3B82F6
    "Dark Mode"

Button onclick toggle

content.text: Now when you click, the button changes color and text. The toggle action cycles through all defined states in order: off → on → off → on...

content.task: Modify the button to have three states: "Low", "Medium", "High" with colors #333, #F59E0B, #EF4444
content.code:
Button: padding 12 radius 8 cursor pointer
  state low
    background #333
    "Low"
  state medium
    background #F59E0B
    "Medium"
  state high
    background #EF4444
    "High"

Button onclick toggle

content.text: Why states instead of variables? You could manage button appearance with variables and conditionals, but states keep all visual variations in one place. You see at a glance every possible appearance of your button – no jumping between files or hunting for where colors are defined.
So far, our button only affects itself. But often you want one element to control another – a button that shows a tooltip, opens a menu, or reveals hidden content.
**Show and Hide**
The simplest way to control another element is show and hide:

content.code:
Box vertical gap 12
  Button background #2271c1 padding 12 radius 8 onclick show Message "Show Message"
  Button background #333 padding 12 radius 8 onclick hide Message "Hide Message"

  Message: hidden background #10B981 padding 12 radius 8 color #fff
    "Hello! I was hidden."

content.text: Notice the hidden property on Message – it starts invisible. The first button shows it, the second hides it.
For toggling visibility with a single button, use toggle with a target:

content.code:
Box vertical gap 12
  Button background #2271c1 padding 12 radius 8 onclick toggle Panel "Toggle Panel"

  Panel: hidden background #1E1E2E padding 16 radius 8 border 1 #333
    "This panel toggles on and off."

content.text: **Multiple Actions**
What if you want one click to do multiple things? Separate actions with a comma:

content.code:
$count: 0

Box vertical gap 12
  Button background #2271c1 padding 12 radius 8 "Do Everything"
    onclick show Message, assign $count to $count + 1

  Message: hidden background #10B981 padding 12 radius 8
    "Shown! Count: " + $count

content.text: Each click shows the message and increments the counter. Actions execute in order, left to right.

items: toggle (no target) – toggle self between states

content.text: We've been using states, but let's understand them properly. A state is a named collection of property overrides. When a state becomes active, its properties replace the defaults.

content.code:
Card: padding 16 background #1E1E2E radius 8 border 1 #333 cursor pointer
  state default
    "Click to select"
  state selected
    border 2 #3B82F6
    background #1E2A4A
    shadow lg
    "Selected!"

Card onclick toggle

content.text: The base Card has bor 1 #333. When the "selected" state activates, it overrides the border to bor 2 #3B82F6 and adds a shadow. Properties not mentioned in the state (like padding and radius) stay the same.
**System States**
Some states are automatic – the browser activates them based on user interaction:

content.code:
Button: padding 12 radius 8 background #333 cursor pointer
  state hover
    background #444
  state active
    background #222

  "Hover and click me"

content.text: You don't need onclick or any action – the browser handles hover and active states automatically.

items: hover – mouse is over the element

content.text: **Hover Shorthand**
For simple hover effects, there's a shorthand that keeps everything on one line:

content.code:
Button: padding 12 radius 8 background #333
  state hover
    background #444
    color #fff
  "Hover me"

Button: padding 12 radius 8 background #333 hover-background #444 hover-color #fff
  "Hover me"

content.text: Available hover shorthands: hover-background, hover-color, hover-border-color, hover-opacity, hover-scale
toggle cycles through states, but sometimes you want to set a specific state. That's what change is for:

content.code:
Status: padding 12 radius 8
  state pending
    background #F59E0B
    "Pending"
  state approved
    background #10B981
    "Approved"
  state rejected
    background #EF4444
    "Rejected"

Box horizontal gap 8
  Button background #333 padding 8 radius 4 onclick change Status to pending "Pending"
  Button background #333 padding 8 radius 4 onclick change Status to approved "Approve"
  Button background #333 padding 8 radius 4 onclick change Status to rejected "Reject"

Status

content.text: Each button sets the Status to a specific state. Use change when you have more than two states or when you need explicit control over which state to activate.
You can also change your own state with self:

content.code:
Tab: padding 12 16 radius 8 cursor pointer
  state inactive
    background transparent color #666
  state active
    background #2271c1 color #fff

Box horizontal gap 4 background #1a1a1a padding 4 radius 8
  Tab onclick change self to active "Tab 1"
  Tab onclick change self to active "Tab 2"
  Tab onclick change self to active "Tab 3"

content.text: But wait – clicking one tab doesn't deactivate the others. We need something more...

subsections.title: Tabs: When Only One Can Be Active

content.text: The tabs above have a problem: you can activate multiple tabs at once. In a real tab bar, selecting one should deselect the others. This is called mutual exclusion.
Mirror provides two actions for this pattern:

items: activate self – set your own state to "active"

content.code:
Tab: padding 12 16 cursor pointer
  state inactive
    color #666
    border b 2 transparent
  state active
    color #fff
    border b 2 #3B82F6

TabBar: horizontal gap 0 border b 1 #333
  - Tab onclick activate self, deactivate-siblings "Overview"
  - Tab onclick activate self, deactivate-siblings "Settings"
  - Tab onclick activate self, deactivate-siblings "History"

content.text: Now only one tab can be active at a time. Click any tab – it activates, and its siblings deactivate.
This pattern works for any mutually exclusive selection: tab bars, toggle groups, radio-like buttons:

content.code:
Option: padding 8 16 radius 4 cursor pointer
  state inactive
    background transparent color #888
  state active
    background #3B82F6 color #fff

ToggleGroup: horizontal gap 2 background #1a1a1a padding 4 radius 8
  - Option onclick activate self, deactivate-siblings "Day"
  - Option onclick activate self, deactivate-siblings "Week"
  - Option onclick activate self, deactivate-siblings "Month"
  - Option onclick activate self, deactivate-siblings "Year"

subsections.title: Highlight vs Select: Menus and Lists

content.text: For menus and dropdowns, you need two different concepts:

items: Highlight – temporary visual feedback (hover, keyboard navigation)

content.text: Think about a dropdown menu: as you move your mouse, items highlight. But they're not selected until you click.

content.code:
Item: padding 8 12 cursor pointer
  state default
    background transparent
  state highlighted
    background #333
  state selected
    background #3B82F6

Menu: vertical background #1E1E2E radius 8 padding 4 width 200
  - Item onhover highlight self, onclick select self "Dashboard"
  - Item onhover highlight self, onclick select self "Settings"
  - Item onhover highlight self, onclick select self "Profile"
  - Item onhover highlight self, onclick select self "Logout"

content.text: Hover over items – they highlight (gray). Click one – it gets selected (blue). The highlight is temporary; the selection persists.

items: highlight next / prev – highlight next/previous sibling
select highlighted – select whatever is currently highlighted

content.text: Good UIs are keyboard accessible. Mirror makes this easy with onkeydown:

content.code:
Item: padding 8 12 cursor pointer
  state default
    background transparent
  state highlighted
    background #333

Menu: vertical background #1E1E2E radius 8 padding 4 width 200
  onkeydown arrow-down highlight next
  onkeydown arrow-up highlight prev
  onkeydown home highlight first
  onkeydown end highlight last

  - Item onhover highlight self "First Item"
  - Item onhover highlight self "Second Item"
  - Item onhover highlight self "Third Item"
  - Item onhover highlight self "Fourth Item"

content.text: Click the menu to focus it, then use arrow keys to navigate. Home jumps to first, End to last.

items: arrow-up, arrow-down, arrow-left, arrow-right – Arrow keys

content.text: **Navigation Targets**
Actions like highlight and focus can target relative positions:

items: self – the element that received the event

content.text: Let's combine everything into a fully functional dropdown with mouse and keyboard support:

content.code:
Item: padding 8 12 cursor pointer
  state default
    background transparent
  state highlighted
    background #333
  state selected
    background #3B82F6

Trigger: padding 8 12 background #1E1E2E radius 8 border 1 #333 cursor pointer
  onclick toggle Dropdown
  "Select an option..."

Dropdown: vertical background #1E1E2E radius 8 shadow lg hidden width 200
  onclick-outside close self
  onkeydown escape close self
  onkeydown arrow-down highlight next
  onkeydown arrow-up highlight prev
  onkeydown enter select highlighted, close self

  - Item onhover highlight self, onclick select self, close Dropdown "Dashboard"
  - Item onhover highlight self, onclick select self, close Dropdown "Settings"
  - Item onhover highlight self, onclick select self, close Dropdown "Profile"

content.text: This dropdown:
Some interactions need timing control. Two common cases:
**Debounce: Wait for User to Stop Typing**
For search/autocomplete, you don't want to filter on every keystroke – wait until the user pauses:

content.code:
$query: ""

Box vertical gap 12 width 300
  Input: padding 8 12 background #1E1E2E radius 8 border 1 #333
    oninput debounce 300 assign $query to $event.value
    "Type something..."

  Text color #666 "Query: " + $query

content.text: debounce 300 waits 300ms after the last keystroke before executing the action. If the user keeps typing, the timer resets.
**Delay: Wait Before Acting**
Sometimes you need a brief pause before an action. Common use: keeping a dropdown open long enough to click it:

content.code:
Box vertical gap 8 width 200
  Input: padding 8 background #1E1E2E radius 8 border 1 #333
    onfocus show Results
    onblur delay 150 hide Results
    "Focus me..."

  Results: vertical background #1E1E2E radius 8 shadow lg hidden
    - Item padding 8 hover-background #333 cursor pointer "Result 1"
    - Item padding 8 hover-background #333 cursor pointer "Result 2"
    - Item padding 8 hover-background #333 cursor pointer "Result 3"

content.text: Without the delay, clicking a result would blur the input and hide the results before the click registers. The 150ms delay gives you time to click.

items: debounce N – wait N ms after last event (resets on each event)
delay N – wait N ms before executing

content.text: Accordions expand and collapse sections. Use toggle-state to flip between two states:

content.code:
Section: vertical border b 1 #333
  Header: horizontal between padding 12 cursor pointer hover-background #1a1a1a
    Title: weight 500
    Icon: icon "chevron-down" color #666
  Content: padding 12 background #1a1a1a

  state collapsed
    Content hidden
  state expanded
    Content visible

Box ver
  Section
    Header onclick toggle-state
      Title "Section 1"
    Content "Content for section 1. Click header to collapse."
  Section collapsed
    Header onclick toggle-state
      Title "Section 2"
    Content "Content for section 2."
  Section collapsed
    Header onclick toggle-state
      Title "Section 3"
    Content "Content for section 3."

content.text: toggle-state cycles through the component's defined states – here, between "collapsed" and "expanded".
Rating components need a special behavior: hovering over star 3 should highlight stars 1, 2, and 3. That's the self-and-before target:

content.code:
Star: cursor pointer
  state empty
    icon "star" color #333 size 24
  state highlighted
    icon "star" color #F59E0B size 24
  state selected
    icon "star" color #F59E0B size 24

Rating: horizontal gap 4
  - Star onhover highlight self-and-before, onclick select self-and-before
  - Star onhover highlight self-and-before, onclick select self-and-before
  - Star onhover highlight self-and-before, onclick select self-and-before
  - Star onhover highlight self-and-before, onclick select self-and-before
  - Star onhover highlight self-and-before, onclick select self-and-before

content.text: Hover over the third star – all three before it (including itself) highlight. Click to make the selection permanent.
**Visibility**
**State Control**
**Selection**
**Data & Navigation**

sections.title: Named Instances
sections.lead: We've learned how events trigger actions. But how do we know what the user entered in a form? How do we target a specific button among many?

content.text: Named instances can also be targeted for styling. Consider a footer with multiple buttons – which one should turn red when you write Footer Button #F00? Mirror can't know. Name them:

content.code:
Footer: padding 16, hor, between, #1a1a1a, radius 8
  Button named Save: #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Save"
  Button named Cancel: #333, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Cancel"

Footer

content.text: Now you can target specific instances:

content.code:
Footer: padding 16, hor, between, #1a1a1a, radius 8
  Button named Save: padding 8 16, radius 6, "Save"
  Button named Cancel: #333, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Cancel"

Footer
  Save #10B981  // Target Save specifically

content.text: Mirror finds Save within Footer and changes its color. Named instances can also be hidden:

content.code:
Footer: padding 16, hor, between, #1a1a1a, radius 8
  Button named Save: #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Save"
  Button named Cancel: #333, padding 8 16, radius 6, "Cancel"

Footer
  Cancel hidden  // Only Save is visible

content.text: Every component has intrinsic properties you can read and write:

content.code:
Input named Email: #1a1a1a, padding 12, radius 8, border 1 #333

Card #1a1a1a, padding 16, radius 8, gap 12
  Email placeholder "Type something..."
  Text #888, size 13
    if Email.value then "You typed: " + Email.value else "Input is empty"
  Button padding 8 16, radius 6
    if Email.value then #2271c1 else #333
    if Email.value then "Submit" else "Enter text first"

content.text: Components ARE the state. No separate state management needed. Email.value is the email – simple as that.

Why this approach? Traditional frameworks require separate state variables (useState) that mirror UI values. In Mirror, the component is the source of truth. This eliminates the "state/UI sync problem" that plagues many applications.

content.task: Create a form where clicking Submit shows an alert with the Email value (use if Email.value to check if it's filled).
content.code:
Input named Email: placeholder "Enter email"
Button named Submit: #2271c1, padding 12, radius 8, "Submit"

Form #3281d1, padding 24, radius 12, gap 16, width 300
  Email
  Submit

sections.title: Overlays
sections.lead: You can now trigger actions and access component properties. A common pattern is showing content that floats above the UI – dialogs, menus, tooltips.

content.text: A modal dialog that blocks interaction with the rest of the page. Define a trigger button and the dialog content:

content.code:
Button onclick open SettingsDialog, "Open Settings"

SettingsDialog as Dialog: background #1A1A23, padding 24, radius 12, width 400
  Title size 18, weight 600, "Settings"
  Text "Configure your preferences here."
  Actions horizontal, gap 8, horizontal-right
    Button background #333, onclick close, "Cancel"
    Button background #2271c1, onclick close, "Save"

content.text:
open SettingsDialog shows the dialog. close hides it.
        Dialogs automatically close when clicking outside or pressing Escape.

content.task: Click the button to open the dialog. Close it with the buttons or click outside.
content.code:
SettingsDialog as Dialog: background #1A1A23, padding 24, radius 12, width 320
  Title size 18, weight 600, "Settings"
  Text color #888, "Configure your preferences here."
  Actions horizontal, gap 8, horizontal-right
    Button background #333, onclick close, "Cancel"
    Button background #2271c1, onclick close, "Save"

Button onclick open SettingsDialog center, "Open Settings"

content.text: Overlays can be positioned relative to their trigger element:

content.code:
Menu as Dropdown:
  Trigger #333, padding 8 12, radius 6
    "Options ▼"
  Content #1a1a1a, padding 4, radius 8, border 1 #333
    Item padding 8 12, radius 4, hover-color #333, "Profile"
    Item "Settings"
    Item #EF4444, "Logout"

Row horizontal, gap 8
  Menu

items: left – to the left of trigger
right – to the right of trigger

content.text: A menu that opens below its trigger:

content.code:
OptionsMenu as Dropdown:
  Trigger: padding 8 12, background #1A1A23, radius 6, border 1 #333
    "Select option"
  Content: ver, background #1A1A23, radius 8, padding 4
    Item: padding 8 12, radius 4, hover-background #333
      "Profile"
    Item "Settings"
    Item "Logout"

content.text: A small hint that appears on hover:

content.code:
HelpTip as Tooltip:
  Trigger
    Button icon help, "?"
  Content: padding 8 12, background #333, radius 6, size 12
    "Click to get help"

content.text: Overlays can have entrance and exit animations:

content.code:
FadeDialog as Dialog: #1a1a1a, padding 24, radius 12, width 250
  show fade 200
  Title weight 600, "Fade Animation"
  Button #333, onclick close, "Close"

ScaleDialog as Dialog: #1a1a1a, padding 24, radius 12, width 250
  show fade scale 200
  Title weight 600, "Scale Animation"
  Button #333, onclick close, "Close"

Row horizontal, gap 8
  Button #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 8, onclick open FadeDialog, "Fade"
  Button #333, padding 8 16, radius 8, onclick open ScaleDialog, "Scale"

content.task: Create a button that opens a dialog. Add a close button inside the dialog.
content.code:
Button #2271c1, padding 12, radius 8, onclick open MyDialog, "Open Dialog"

MyDialog as Dialog: #1A1A23, padding 24, radius 12, width 300, ver, gap 16
  Title size 18, weight 600, "Hello!"
  Text "This is a dialog."
  Button #333, onclick close, "Close"

sections.title: Animations
sections.lead: Overlays appear and disappear. Animations smooth the transitions between visibility states.

content.text: Use show and hide blocks to define how an element animates in and out:

content.code:
Card #1a1a1a, padding 16, radius 8, gap 12
  Button #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 6, onclick toggle Panel, "Toggle Panel"
  Panel hidden, #333, padding 16, radius 8
    show fade slide-up 300
    hide fade 150
    Text "This panel fades and slides!"

content.text:
The panel starts hidden (hidden). When it becomes visible, it fades in and slides up (300ms).
        When hiding, it fades out (200ms). Animations can be combined: fade slide-up.

content.code:
Card #1a1a1a, padding 16, radius 8, gap 12
  Button #333, padding 8 16, radius 6, onclick toggle Box, "Toggle Animation"
  Box hidden, hor, gap 8
    show fade slide-up 300
    Tile #2271c1, 60 60, radius 8, cen, "fade"
    Tile #10B981, 60 60, radius 8, cen, "slide"
    Tile #EF4444, 60 60, radius 8, cen, "scale"

content.text: ⚠️ **Note:** Continuous animations (animate spin, pulse, bounce) are NOT YET IMPLEMENTED in the current parser. The syntax below is documented for future use.

content.code:
Loader 24 24
  animate spin 1000
  Icon icon loader

content.text: When implemented, the icon will rotate continuously, completing one full rotation every 1000ms. Currently only show/hide animations (fade, slide-up, etc.) work.
An expandable card with animations:

content.code:
Card background #1A1A23, padding 16, radius 8
  Header hor, between, vertical-center, onclick toggle Details
    Title weight 600, "Click to expand"
    Icon icon chevron-down
  Details hidden
    show fade slide-down 200
    hide fade 150
    Text padding u 12, "Here are the details..."

content.task: Add a show animation to make the content fade in when revealed.
content.code:
Card #1A1A23, padding 16, radius 8, ver, gap 12
  Button #333, onclick toggle Content, "Toggle Content"
  Content hidden
    Text "This content can be toggled"

sections.title: Variables & State
sections.lead: You've built static interfaces – components that always look the same. Now let's make them come alive. A button that counts clicks. A toggle that remembers its state. A display that updates in real-time.

content.text: Variables start with $ and are defined with a colon:

content.code:
$count: 0
$name: "World"
$isActive: true

Card background #1A1A23, padding 16, radius 8, ver, gap 8
  Text "Count: " + $count
  Text "Hello, " + $name
  Text if $isActive then "Active" else "Inactive"

content.text: Variables can hold numbers, text, or boolean values (true/false). Use + to combine text with variables.
Use assign in event handlers to change a variable's value:

content.code:
$count: 0

Counter hor, gap 16, vertical-center, background #1A1A23, padding 16, radius 8
  Button background #333, padding 8 16, radius 6, onclick assign $count to $count - 1, "-"
  Text size 24, weight 600, width 60, align center, $count
  Button background #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 6, onclick assign $count to $count + 1, "+"

content.text: Click the buttons – the count updates instantly. The Text component automatically reflects the new value because it displays $count.
A common pattern is toggling between two states. Use not to flip a boolean:

content.code:
$liked: false

LikeButton hor, gap 8, vertical-center, padding 12 16, radius 8, cursor pointer
  if $liked then background #DC2626 else background #333
  onclick assign $liked to not $liked
  Icon icon heart, size 18
  Text if $liked then "Liked" else "Like"

content.text: Mirror supports basic math: +, -, *, /

content.code:
$price: 29
$quantity: 2

Cart background #1A1A23, padding 16, radius 8, ver, gap 12
  Row horizontal, between
    Text "Price:"
    Text "$" + $price
  Row horizontal, between
    Text "Quantity:"
    Row horizontal, gap 8
      Button background #333, padding 4 12, radius 4, onclick assign $quantity to $quantity - 1, "-"
      Text width 30, align center, $quantity
      Button background #333, padding 4 12, radius 4, onclick assign $quantity to $quantity + 1, "+"
  Divider height 1, background #333
  Row horizontal, between
    Text weight 600, "Total:"
    Text weight 600, color #10B981, "$" + ($price * $quantity)

content.task: Create a temperature converter. Define $celsius: 20 and display Fahrenheit ($celsius * 9 / 5 + 32).
content.code:
$celsius: 20

Converter background #1A1A23, padding 16, radius 8, ver, gap 12
  Row horizontal, gap 8, vertical-center
    Button background #333, padding 8, radius 4, onclick assign $celsius to $celsius - 5, "-5"
    Text size 24, weight 600, $celsius + "°C"
    Button background #333, padding 8, radius 4, onclick assign $celsius to $celsius + 5, "+5"
  // Add Fahrenheit display here

content.text: Variables make your UI reactive. Change a value once, and everything that depends on it updates automatically. This is the foundation for dynamic interfaces.

sections.title: Conditional Display
sections.lead: Your UI responds to clicks and updates values. But what if you want to show completely different content based on state? A login button that becomes a logout button. An error message that only appears when needed. A theme that switches between light and dark.

content.text: Change a single property based on a condition with if ... then ... else:

content.code:
$isOnline: true

Status hor, gap 8, vertical-center, background #1A1A23, padding 12, radius 8
  Dot width 8, height 8, radius 4
    if $isOnline then background #10B981 else background #666
  Text if $isOnline then "Online" else "Offline"
  Button background #333, padding 6 12, radius 4, size 12
    onclick assign $isOnline to not $isOnline
    "Toggle"

content.text: The dot and text both respond to $isOnline. One condition, multiple effects.
When you want to show different components entirely, use if / else blocks:

content.code:
$loggedIn: false

Header hor, between, vertical-center, background #1A1A23, padding 16, radius 8
  Logo weight 600, "MyApp"
  if $loggedIn
    Row horizontal, gap 12, vertical-center
      Avatar width 32, height 32, radius 16, background #2271c1
      Button background #333, padding 8 16, radius 6
        onclick assign $loggedIn to false
        "Logout"
  else
    Button background #2271c1, padding 8 16, radius 6
      onclick assign $loggedIn to true
      "Login"

content.text: The if block shows the avatar and logout button when logged in. The else block shows the login button otherwise. Completely different UI structures, controlled by one variable.
Conditions can compare values: == (equal), != (not equal), >, <, >=, <=

content.code:
$score: 75

Result background #1A1A23, padding 16, radius 8, ver, gap 12
  Row horizontal, gap 8, vertical-center
    Text "Score:"
    Button background #333, padding 4 8, radius 4, onclick assign $score to $score - 10, "-10"
    Text weight 600, size 20, $score
    Button background #333, padding 4 8, radius 4, onclick assign $score to $score + 10, "+10"
  Badge padding 8 16, radius 6
    if $score >= 90 then background #10B981 else if $score >= 70 then background #F59E0B else background #EF4444
    if $score >= 90 then "Excellent" else if $score >= 70 then "Good" else "Needs Work"

content.text: Combine conditions with and, or, and not:

content.code:
$hasEmail: true
$hasPassword: false

Form background #1A1A23, padding 16, radius 8, ver, gap 12
  Row horizontal, gap 8
    Checkbox width 18, height 18, radius 4
      if $hasEmail then background #2271c1 else background #333
      onclick assign $hasEmail to not $hasEmail
    Text "Email entered"
  Row horizontal, gap 8
    Checkbox width 18, height 18, radius 4
      if $hasPassword then background #2271c1 else background #333
      onclick assign $hasPassword to not $hasPassword
    Text "Password entered"
  Button padding 12, radius 6, width full
    if $hasEmail and $hasPassword then background #2271c1 else background #333
    if $hasEmail and $hasPassword then "Submit" else "Fill all fields"

content.task: Create a theme switcher with a $darkMode variable. Change the card background between #1A1A23 (dark) and #F5F5F5 (light).
content.code:
$darkMode: true

Card padding 24, radius 12, ver, gap 16
  if $darkMode then background #1A1A23 else background #F5F5F5
  Title size 20, weight 600
    if $darkMode then color white else color #1A1A23
    "Theme Demo"
  Text if $darkMode then color #9CA3AF else color #666
    "Click to switch themes"
  Button padding 12, radius 6
    if $darkMode then background #2271c1 else background #1A1A23
    onclick assign $darkMode to not $darkMode
    if $darkMode then "Switch to Light" else "Switch to Dark"

content.text: Conditions turn static layouts into adaptive interfaces. The same code handles multiple states – you just describe what each state looks like.

sections.title: Lists & Data
sections.lead: You can show and hide elements, change their properties. But what about a list of 50 products? 100 messages? You're not going to type each one. Real apps get data – from APIs, databases, user input – and generate UI from it.

content.text: Use each $item in $list to create a component for every item:

content.code:
$fruits: ["Apple", "Banana", "Cherry", "Date"]

List ver, gap 8
  each $fruit in $fruits
    Item background #1A1A23, padding 12, radius 6, $fruit

content.text: Four items in the array, four components rendered. The variable $fruit holds each value in turn.
Real data usually has structure. Access properties with dot notation:

content.code:
$users: [
  { name: "Alice", role: "Admin", online: true },
  { name: "Bob", role: "Editor", online: true },
  { name: "Carol", role: "Viewer", online: false }
]

UserList ver, gap 8
  each $user in $users
    Card hor, between, vertical-center, background #1A1A23, padding 12, radius 8
      Left hor, gap 12, vertical-center
        Status width 8, height 8, radius 4
          if $user.online then background #10B981 else background #666
        Name weight 500, $user.name
      Role color #888, size 13, $user.role

content.text: Iterate within iterations for hierarchical data:

content.code:
$menu: [
  { category: "Starters", items: ["Soup", "Salad", "Bread"] },
  { category: "Mains", items: ["Pasta", "Steak", "Fish"] },
  { category: "Desserts", items: ["Cake", "Ice Cream"] }
]

Menu ver, gap 16
  each $section in $menu
    Section ver, gap 8
      Title weight 600, color #2271c1, $section.category
      Items ver, gap 4
        each $item in $section.items
          Item background #1A1A23, padding 8 12, radius 6, $item

content.text: Capture user input with $event.value:

content.code:
$search: ""

$items: ["Dashboard", "Settings", "Profile", "Messages", "Help"]

Search ver, gap 12, width 250
  Input background #1A1A23, padding 12, radius 8, border 1 #333
    placeholder "Search..."
    oninput assign $search to $event.value
  Results ver, gap 4
    each $item in $items
      Item background #1A1A23, padding 8 12, radius 6, $item

content.text: Filter displayed items by combining each with if:

content.code:
$tasks: [
  { title: "Design mockup", done: true },
  { title: "Build prototype", done: false },
  { title: "User testing", done: false },
  { title: "Write docs", done: true }
]
$showDone: true

TaskList ver, gap 12
  Row horizontal, gap 8, vertical-center
    Checkbox width 18, height 18, radius 4
      if $showDone then background #2271c1 else background #333
      onclick assign $showDone to not $showDone
    Text size 13, color #888, "Show completed"
  Tasks ver, gap 8
    each $task in $tasks
      if $showDone or not $task.done
        Task hor, gap 12, vertical-center, background #1A1A23, padding 12, radius 8
          Icon if $task.done then color #10B981 else color #333
            icon if $task.done then check-circle else circle
          Text if $task.done then color #666 else color white, $task.title

content.task: Create a contact list with name, email, and a status indicator. Use $contact.active to show green/gray dots.

content.text: Data-driven UIs scale effortlessly. Whether you have 3 items or 3000, the same template handles them all. The UI becomes a view of your data.

sections.title: Data Tab
sections.lead: For complex prototypes with structured data, the Data tab provides a cleaner way to define schemas and instances separately from your layout code.

content.text: A schema defines the structure of your data. Use a colon after the type name, then indent fields with their types:

content.code:
// In the Data tab:
Task:
  title: text
  done: boolean
  priority: number

Category:
  name: text
  color: text

content.text: Each field has a name and a type. Available types are:
After defining schemas, create instances using - prefix. Values are listed in field order:

content.code:
// Schema
Category:
  name: text
  color: text

// Instances (values in field order, comma-separated)
- Category "Arbeit", "#3B82F6"
- Category "Privat", "#10B981"
- Category "Dringend", "#EF4444"

content.text: For schemas with many fields:

content.code:
Task:
  title: text
  done: boolean
  priority: number

// Instances: title, done, priority (comma-separated)
- Task "Einkaufen", false, 1
- Task "Sport machen", true, 2
- Task "Meeting vorbereiten", false, 3

content.text: Use index notation to reference other instances. Category[0] refers to the first Category instance:

content.code:
Category:
  name: text

Task:
  title: text
  done: boolean
  category: Category    // Reference to Category type

// Categories
- Category "Arbeit"
- Category "Privat"

// Tasks referencing categories by index (comma-separated)
- Task "Email schreiben", false, Category[0]    // References "Arbeit"
- Task "Joggen gehen", true, Category[1]        // References "Privat"
- Task "Report erstellen", false, Category[0]   // References "Arbeit"

content.text: The index [0] refers to the first instance of that type, [1] to the second, and so on.
Use the data property to bind a container to your data. The container repeats its children for each record:
Note: Field binding syntax (Tasks.title) to display data values directly is planned for a future release.
Here's a full example with multiple related types:

content.code:
// ===== DATA TAB =====

// Schema definitions
Category:
  name: text
  icon: text
  color: text

Task:
  title: text
  done: boolean
  category: Category

// Category instances
- Category "Work", "briefcase", "#3B82F6"
- Category "Personal", "user", "#10B981"
- Category "Urgent", "alert-circle", "#EF4444"

// Task instances
- Task "Prepare presentation", false, Category[0]
- Task "Buy birthday gift", false, Category[1]
- Task "Fix critical bug", false, Category[2]
- Task "Morning workout", true, Category[1]
- Task "Team meeting", true, Category[0]

// ===== LAYOUT TAB =====

// The container repeats for each Task record
TaskList data Tasks ver gap 8 padding 16
  Card hor between vertical-center bg #1A1A23 padding 12 radius 8
    Left hor gap 12 vertical-center
      Icon icon "file-text"
      Title "Task item"
    Status
      Icon icon "circle" color #333

// With filter: only show incomplete tasks
TodoList data Tasks where done == false ver gap 8
  Card background #1A1A23 padding 12 radius 8
    Text "Todo item"

content.text: When you've defined schemas in the Data tab, a Generate button appears. Click it to have AI create realistic sample data based on your schema definitions.
The AI understands your field names and types, generating contextually appropriate values:

items: email: text → Generates realistic email addresses
price: number → Generates appropriate price values

content.text: This is especially useful for prototyping: define your data structure once, then generate 10, 20, or 50 realistic entries instantly.

sections.title: Interactive Components
sections.lead: You've built data-driven interfaces. Now let's add sophisticated interaction patterns – keyboard navigation, click-outside-to-close, selection states. The kind of behavior that makes a dropdown feel polished.

content.text: Beyond system states like hover, you can define custom states that change through actions:

content.code:
Item: padding 12 16, radius 6, cursor pointer
  state default
    background transparent
  state highlighted
    background #333
  state selected
    background #2271c1

Menu ver, gap 2, background #1A1A23, padding 8, radius 8, width 200
  Item onhover highlight self, "Dashboard"
  Item onhover highlight self, "Settings"
  Item onhover highlight self, "Profile"

content.text: highlight self activates the highlighted state. The state defines what changes visually. Hover over the items to see it in action.
Handle keyboard events with onkeydown plus a key name:

content.code:
Item: padding 12 16, radius 6, cursor pointer
  state default
    background transparent
  state highlighted
    background #333

Menu ver, gap 2, background #1A1A23, padding 8, radius 8, width 200
  onkeydown arrow-down highlight next
  onkeydown arrow-up highlight prev
  Item "Dashboard"
  Item "Settings"
  Item "Profile"
  Item "Logout"

content.text: Click the menu to focus it, then use arrow keys. highlight next and highlight prev move through siblings automatically.
Close dropdowns when clicking elsewhere with onclick-outside:

content.code:
Trigger: background #2271c1, padding 12 16, radius 8, cursor pointer
  onclick toggle Dropdown

Dropdown: ver, background #1A1A23, padding 8, radius 8, width 180, hidden, shadow lg
  onclick-outside close self
  Item: padding 10 14, radius 6, cursor pointer, hover-background #333

Box ver, gap 8
  Trigger "Open Menu"
  Dropdown
    Item "Edit"
    Item "Duplicate"
    Item color #EF4444, "Delete"

content.text: Use select for persistent selection that survives closing:

content.code:
Item: padding 12 16, radius 6, cursor pointer
  state default
    background transparent
  state highlighted
    background #333
  state selected
    background #2271c1
  onclick select self
  onhover highlight self

Menu ver, gap 2, background #1A1A23, padding 8, radius 8, width 200
  Item "Option A"
  Item "Option B"
  Item "Option C"

content.text: highlight is temporary (for navigation). select is persistent (for choosing).
Combining everything into a production-ready pattern:

content.code:
// The trigger button
Trigger: background #1A1A23, padding 12 16, radius 8, border 1 #333, hor, between, vertical-center, width 200, cursor pointer
  onclick toggle Dropdown
  Text "Select option..."
  Icon icon chevron-down, size 14, color #888

// Menu items with three states
Item: padding 10 14, radius 6, cursor pointer
  state default
    background transparent
  state highlighted
    background #333
  state selected
    background #2271c1
  onclick select self, close Dropdown
  onhover highlight self

// The dropdown with keyboard support
Dropdown: ver, background #1A1A23, padding 6, radius 8, width 200, hidden, shadow lg, border 1 #333
  onclick-outside close self
  onkeydown escape close self
  onkeydown arrow-down highlight next
  onkeydown arrow-up highlight prev
  onkeydown enter select highlighted, close self

// Usage
Select ver, gap 4
  Trigger
  Dropdown
    Item icon home, "Dashboard"
    Item icon user, "Profile"
    Item icon settings, "Settings"
    Item icon log-out, color #EF4444, "Logout"

content.text: ~25 lines for a fully accessible dropdown with keyboard navigation, click-outside, and selection states. The behavior reads like documentation: "on keydown arrow-down, highlight next."
When interactions grow complex, separate structure from behavior with the events block:

content.code:
// === COMPONENTS ===
Input Email: placeholder "Email", padding 12, radius 8, background #1A1A23, border 1 #333
Input Password: placeholder "Password", type password, padding 12, radius 8, background #1A1A23, border 1 #333
Error: color #EF4444, size 13
Button Submit: background #2271c1, padding 12, radius 8, "Login"

$error: ""

// === LAYOUT ===
LoginForm ver, gap 12, padding 24, background #0F0F14, radius 12, width 280
  Input Email
  Input Password
  if $error
    Error $error
  Button Submit

// === BEHAVIOR ===
events
  Email onchange
    assign $error to ""
  Password onchange
    assign $error to ""
  Submit onclick
    if Email.value and Password.value
      assign $error to ""
      Submit.label = "Success!"
    else
      assign $error to "Please fill all fields"

content.text: Structure at the top, behavior at the bottom. Each section has one job. As forms grow, this separation keeps code maintainable.

content.task: Build a tab component: three tab buttons that highlight on hover and select on click. Use state highlighted and state selected.
content.code:
Tab: padding 12 24, cursor pointer
  state default
    background transparent, bor-b 2, border-color transparent
  state highlighted
    border-color #333
  state selected
    border-color #2271c1
  onclick select self
  onhover highlight self

Tabs hor, background #1A1A23, radius 8
  Tab "Overview"
  Tab "Details"
  Tab "Settings"

content.text: Behaviors are declarative. You describe what should happen, not how. Mirror handles event listeners, state management, focus handling, and cleanup automatically.

sections.title: Quick Reference
sections.lead: All properties, keywords, and patterns at a glance.

items: grid auto 250 – auto-fill with min width
pad l 12 – left only (also: r, u, d, l-r, u-d)
col #2271c1 – unified color (background for containers, text for text)
bor l 2 dashed #333 – directional compound border
shadow sm / md / lg – box shadow
onkeydown escape / enter / arrow-down / arrow-up – key events
highlight self / next / prev – navigation highlight
filter Container – filter items by input
if $x then prop value else prop value – conditional property
Types: fade, scale, slide-up/down/left/right, spin, pulse, bounce
Email as Input: – named input with type inference
Accordion – AccordionItem, Trigger, Content (states: collapsed, expanded)
Dropdown – Trigger, Content, Item, Separator (states: closed, open)
AlertDialog – Trigger, Content, Title, Description, Cancel, Action
Select – Trigger, Options, Option, Group, Label
RadioGroup – Item, Indicator, Label (states: unselected, selected)
Slider – Track, Range, Thumb (props: min, max, value, step)
change self to state – Set own state explicitly
change Component to state – Set another component's state
onclick if cond action else action – Conditional action
